era_context: The mid-14th century: the Hundred Years' War is in its early phase; the Black Death is spreading along trade routes; the Yuan Dynasty is fragmenting; the Mamluk Sultanate dominates the Eastern Mediterranean; and European feudalism is under structural stress from plague, war, and economic change.

events.title: Clement VI's early papacy and Avignon consolidation
events.description: Pope Clement VI (elected 1342) was consolidating papal authority and patronage in Avignon in 1343. He was known for his support of the arts, his diplomatic acumen, and his later response to the plague. In 1343, he was establishing his court and building relationships with European powers. His papacy would be marked by efforts to maintain papal independence and influence during the Hundred Years' War.

events.title: Hundred Years' War: English-French tensions escalate
events.description: In 1343, Edward III of England was consolidating his claim to the French throne and preparing for military campaigns. The Hundred Years' War (1337–1453) was in its early phase. In 1343, there were skirmishes and diplomatic maneuvering, but no major pitched battles. The conflict was driven by Edward III's claim to the French throne (through his mother Isabella) and competition for control of Aquitaine and other French territories.
events.certainty_note: The broader conflict is well-documented; specific events in 1343 are less detailed.

events.title: Black Death spreads in Asia; plague in Central Asia and China
events.description: The bubonic plague (Yersinia pestis) was spreading along Silk Road trade routes in 1343. Chinese sources document plague deaths in Central Asia and possibly in China itself during this period. The exact chronology of plague spread is debated by historians, but by 1343, the pandemic was well-established in Asia and moving westward. The disease would reach the Mediterranean by 1347.

sources.name: Yuan Shi (History of the Yuan) and Chinese local records

events.certainty_note: Plague's presence in Asia by 1343 is supported by Chinese sources, though exact dating varies by region.

events.title: Yuan Dynasty instability; Toghon Temür's reign
events.description: In 1343, the Yuan Dynasty under Emperor Shun (Toghon Temür, r. 1333–1368) was experiencing severe internal conflict. Factional struggles among Mongol nobility, Han Chinese officials, and regional warlords were destabilizing the empire. Famine and peasant unrest were growing. The empire would face the Red Turban Rebellion (1351–1368) and ultimately collapse to the Ming Dynasty (1368). In 1343, the Yuan was still nominally powerful but increasingly fragile.
key_figures: Toghon Temür (Emperor Shun of Yuan, r. 1333–1368)

sources.name: Yuan Shi (History of the Yuan, compiled by Ming historians)
Chinese local records and administrative documents

events.certainty_note: Yuan instability in the 1340s is well-documented in Chinese sources, though specific events in 1343 are less detailed.

events.title: Mali Empire under Mansa Sulayman; West African trade networks
events.description: In 1343, Mali was ruled by Mansa Sulayman (r. c. 1341–1360), who succeeded Mansa Maghan I. Sulayman was known for his piety and his maintenance of Mali's Islamic institutions and trade networks. The empire was still a major power in West African gold trade, though it was beginning to face competition from rising Songhai and other regional powers. In 1343, Mali's control over trans-Saharan trade routes was still strong but increasingly contested.

sources.name: Tarikh al-Sudan and Tarikh al-Fattash (West African chronicles)

events.certainty_note: Mali's political history in the 1340s relies on oral tradition and later chronicles. Specific events in 1343 are not well-documented.

disconfirming_evidence: Mali's rulers in the 1340s are often conflated or misnamed in Western sources. The exact succession and dates of Mansa Maghan I and Mansa Sulayman are disputed. Yuan Dynasty instability is sometimes overstated; the empire retained significant administrative capacity and military power even in the 1340s. The plague's spread in 1343 is less well-documented than in 1344–1347; some historians argue the pandemic's major impact in Asia occurred slightly later.
historiographic_note: 1343 is a year of gradual change rather than dramatic events. European sources focus on the Hundred Years' War and papal politics. Chinese sources document Yuan instability. West African history relies on oral tradition and later chronicles, with significant gaps. The plague's spread is documented retrospectively rather than in contemporary 1343 records. Bias toward European and East Asian history is evident.

graph_edges.note: English military preparations in 1343 led to the Battle of Crécy in 1346
Plague spread in Asia in 1343 reached the Mediterranean in 1347
Yuan instability in 1343 contributed to the Red Turban Rebellion (1351)
